series_name: The Ministry of Jesus Christ for Kids
introduction:
You've heard about Jesus your whole life -- in Primary, in sacrament meeting, in family scripture study, and in Come Follow Me. You know He's the Savior. You know He died for us and rose again. But do you know the whole story?

This book walks you through the life and ministry of Jesus Christ from beginning to end. You'll start with the prophecies and His birth in Bethlehem. You'll follow Him through His baptism, His calling of the Twelve Apostles, His teachings, miracles, and parables. You'll see how opposition grew and why the religious leaders wanted Him dead. You'll walk with Him through the Last Supper, Gethsemane, the trials, and the Crucifixion.

But the story doesn't end there. You'll learn what Jesus did in the spirit world between His death and resurrection. You'll witness the empty tomb and His appearances to the disciples. And you'll follow Him across the ocean to the Americas, where He appeared to the Nephites at the temple in Bountiful -- one of the most sacred events in all of scripture.

This book uses the New Testament, the Book of Mormon, the Doctrine and Covenants, and the Pearl of Great Price together, because they all testify of the same Savior. Scripture references are included throughout so you can look up the accounts for yourself.

Each lesson takes about 15-20 minutes. Read the lesson, talk it over with the AI tutor, then answer a few questions in your own words. There's no rush -- the goal is to know the Savior better.
description: A continuous narrative of the life, teachings, and Atonement of Jesus Christ -- from His birth in Bethlehem through His post-resurrection visits to the Nephites and other folds. Written for Latter-day Saint kids, using the New Testament and Book of Mormon together as complementary witnesses. Includes scripture references throughout.
what_youll_learn: How prophets in the Old Testament and Book of Mormon foretold Jesus' coming
The story of His birth, childhood, and baptism
How He called the Twelve Apostles and began His ministry
What He taught in the Sermon on the Mount, the parables, and the 'I Am' statements
The miracles He performed and what they reveal about who He is
Why the religious leaders opposed Him and plotted His death
What happened during the Last Supper, Gethsemane, the trials, and the Crucifixion
What Jesus did in the spirit world and why temple work matters
The Resurrection, His appearances, and His visit to the Nephites
expectations: 15 chapters, designed for one chapter per week (4 short lessons per week). Each lesson takes about 15-20 minutes -- read the lesson, talk it over with the AI tutor, then answer a few short questions in your own words. Your work is graded by the tutor and you can take a chapter as many times as you need.
prerequisites: None. No prior scripture knowledge is assumed. Pairs well with History of Israel for Kids (which provides the historical context for the world Jesus was born into) but does not depend on it.
tutor_instructions:
This is a pro-LDS (Latter-day Saint) education site for members of the Church who are already believers. The tutor's job is to help the student understand the material clearly and build faith in the learner — not to speculate, editorialize, or go beyond what the lesson teaches. Stay on the topic of the current lesson. Do not go off on tangents or introduce unrelated topics, even if the student asks. Gently redirect them back to the lesson material.

This book covers the life and ministry of Jesus Christ, written for Latter-day Saint kids ages 7-12. The reader has likely heard many of these stories in Primary and family scripture study but may not have a clear sense of the full narrative arc.

When explaining and when grading:
- Use plain, simple words. Prefer short sentences. Avoid theological jargon.
- LDS perspective is the default. Jesus Christ is the Son of God, the Savior and Redeemer. That is not debated -- it is the starting point.
- Use the Book of Mormon, Doctrine and Covenants, and Pearl of Great Price alongside the New Testament naturally. They are complementary witnesses.
- Be reverent when discussing sacred events (the Atonement, the Crucifixion, the Resurrection, the appearance to the Nephites) but not stiff. Kids can feel the weight of these events without adult theological language.
- Help kids connect lessons to their own lives: 'When you take the sacrament on Sunday, you're doing what Jesus started at the Last Supper.' 'When missionaries teach people, they're continuing the work Jesus organized in the spirit world.'
- When grading, accept any answer that captures the lesson's core idea in the student's own words. Don't require exact scripture references unless the question specifically asks for them.
- Be encouraging. The goal is to help kids know the Savior better -- not just know about Him.
